variants.llm_generated: Geoffrey Chaucer was a renowned English author, philosopher, alchemist, and astronomer. He is best known for writing 'The Canterbury Tales,' but he also composed a scientific treatise on the astrolabe for his son. In addition to his literary achievements, Chaucer held various positions in the civil service, working as a bureaucrat, courtier, and diplomat. He played a significant role in establishing Middle English as a legitimate literary language at a time when French and Latin were dominant in England.
variants.llm_humanized: Chaucer did quite a lot—he wasn’t just the guy who wrote 'The Canterbury Tales.' He was also an author, philosopher, alchemist, and even dabbled in astronomy, writing a treatise on the astrolabe for his son. On top of that, he worked in the civil service as a bureaucrat, courtier, and diplomat. Importantly, he helped make Middle English a respected language for literature, which was a big deal since French and Latin were the main literary languages back then.
variants.human_polished: While Chaucer achieved fame during his lifetime as an author, philosopher, alchemist, and astronomer—including composing a scientific treatise on the astrolabe for his ten-year-old son Lewis—he also maintained an active career in the civil service as a bureaucrat, courtier, and diplomat. Chaucer is a crucial figure in developing the legitimacy of the vernacular, Middle English, at a time when the dominant literary languages in England were French and Latin.
variants.dictated_spoken: So, Chaucer did a lot of things—he was famous in his own time as an author, philosopher, alchemist, and even an astronomer. He actually wrote a scientific piece about the astrolabe for his son Lewis, who was ten at the time. Besides all that, he worked in the civil service, you know, as a bureaucrat, courtier, and diplomat. And he’s really important for making Middle English a legitimate language for literature, especially since French and Latin were the main ones used in England back then.
